issue_title: Hidden package caching behavior breaks package exclusion
issue_body:
The `get_package_info` command provides an optional parameter `exclude=`, which tells `setuptools` to ignore packages with names that match certain patterns. For example, a common use case would be to avoid installing tests:

```python
# setup.py
import ah_bootstrap
from setuptools import setup
from astropy_helpers.setup_helpers import get_package_info

...

package_info = get_package_info(exclude=['*.tests'])
setup(**package_info)
```

In many (if not all) cases, a project will also want to use custom commands provided by `astropy_helpers`. To do this, the code ends up looking like the following:

```python
# setup.py
import ah_bootstrap
from setuptools import setup
from astropy_helpers.setup_helpers import get_package_info, register_commands

...

release = False
cmdclassd = register_commands('foobar', '1.0.dev', release)
package_info = get_package_info(exclude=['*.tests'])
setup(cmdclass=cmdclassd, **package_info)
```

Here's the problem: when I call `register_commands`, it implicitly calls the underlying `find_packages` routine, but without any knowledge of the packages that I want to exclude. It then does me the favor of caching the packages that it found so that it doesn't have to search again. However, when I call `get_package_info`, it goes straight to the cached list of packages and does not filter against my requested exclusions.

I think I understand the issue well enough so I should be able to submit a PR with a fix sooner or later. Any input on the right approach is welcome, though. Right now it's not clear to me whether `register_commands` actually needs to know anything about the listed packages or not. If it doesn't, that would be an easy and clean fix.

issue_title: TypeError: Object of type 'bytes' is not JSON serializable (encoding raw_response.user_presence)
issue_body: uf2_v2.py's `verify_authenticate` returns raw_response.user_presence, which will then be passed to `json.dumps` as the touch value. This fails on py3 as the input type is `bytes`. I'm unsure why it appears to work in the instructions (additionally, the instructions show it as a raw unicode character, which seems undesirable). This value should just be converted to an integer before passing to `json.dumps`.

issue_title: Is it worth a Conda-specific compatibility note?
issue_body:
I was attempting to help some visiting astronomers start using pipenv and ran into two problems with the instructions:

https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/docs/advanced.rst#-pipenv-and-conda

The easy problem was that `/path/to/anaconda…` will not work if you simply copy and paste it but it's possible to miss that because while it prints “The path /foo/bar/baaz (from --python=/foo/bar/baaz) does not exist” that is buried under a lengthy traceback with the last thing people see being “TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable”. Do you think it'd be worth a pull-request which would just end with the first message?

The more time-consuming problem was actually a Conda problem where the virtualenv creation failed with an error message early in the startup about being unable to `import _io` in the tempfile stdlib module, which was fixed at some point but hadn't been updated on their system. `conda update python virtualenv` fixed the problem but wasn't the first thing they were jumping to try. Should I contribute a brief note for the docs suggesting this more obviously?

issue_title: When a virtualenv is already active, `pipenv shell` suggests using `pew new`
issue_body:
### To Reproduce:
1. Activate a virtual env using virtualenv or virtualenvwrapper
2. Go to a folder with no Pipenv/pipfile
3. `$ pipenv shell` 

### Result:

Raises
```
ERROR: Environment 'folder-1AY1GkYk' does not exist. Create it with 'pew new folder-1AY1GkYk'.
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9513968/39495126-0c0c4cfc-4d67-11e8-9f3a-13de2073a1b0.png)

### Expected
An error about a virtualenv already being active. Perhaps similar to the error shown when using 
`$ pipenv --three`, which issues a courtesy warning:
'Pipenv found itself running within a virtual environment ... '
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9513968/39495138-1af05be6-4d67-11e8-86bb-acf32a49980c.png)

### Note

While I understand pipenv can't always play nice with every possible virtual env configurations it might find itself one, standard virtualenv is still widely used and until I can migrate all projects I still find myself jumping between the two and making these types of mistakes often. Hopefuly I am not the only one : )

These courtesy messages are incredibly helpful and can save users and maintainers troubleshooting time

issue_title: Environment variables are stored in plain text when installing packages from a private repository
issue_body:
[Injecting credentials into Pipfiles via environment variables](https://docs.pipenv.org/advanced/#injecting-credentials-into-pipfiles-via-environment-variables) works as advertised when doing `pipenv install`. However, it expands and stores the environment variables in both the `Pipfile` and the `Pipfile.lock` files when installing a package via `pipenv install package-name`.

<details><summary>$ python -m pipenv.help output (partial)</summary>

[packages]

[requires]

issue_title: Support extras that reference other extras
issue_body:
I want to do something like this (simplified for the example):

```py
setup(
    name='my-project',
    extras_require={
        'tester': [
            "pytest==3.3.2",
        ],
        'dev': [
            "pytest-xdist",
            ".[tester]",
        ],
    },
    ...
)
```

When I run `pip install -e .[dev]`, I want it to install pytest and xdist. Right now, it fails saying that I supplied an invalid package name.

Also of interest is that the following incantation spits no error, but seems to ignore the tester extra:

```
        'dev': [

Package metadata is standardized and structured data that is often reused across projects, (such as in `setup.py` and `--help` output). `<pkg_resources.EggInfoDistribution>` supports retrieving this information via `get_metadata()`, but returns only a text blob. This has lead to projects re-implementing parsing logic in their codebases (i.e. [mozilla/gecko-dev/.../utils.py#L20](https://github.com/mozilla/gecko-dev/blob/master/testing/mozbase/mozrunner/mozrunner/utils.py#L20)). Package data should instead be exposed as a dictionary or first-class object.

issue_title: test command prevents other commands from running
issue_body:
The test command seems to prevent other commands from running. Looks like this issue was introduced in 18.4; in 18.3.2 it doesn't occur. Using https://github.com/pypa/sampleproject:

Setup:

```
$ pyvenv-3.5 venv
$ source venv/bin/activate
```

With setuptools 18.3.2, both "setup.by build test" and "setup.py test build" run the specified commands in the expected order:

```
$ pip install setuptools==18.3.2
Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): setuptools==18.3.2 in ./venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages
$ python setup.py build test
running build
[...]
running test
[...]
$ python setup.py test build
running test
[...]
running build
[...]
```

...but with 18.4 (and up to 28.8.0), "setup.py build test" runs both build and test, but "setup.py test build" does **not** run build at all.

```
$ pip install setuptools==18.4
[...]
Successfully installed setuptools-18.4
$ python setup.py build test
running build
[...]
running test
[...]
$ python setup.py test build
running test
[...]
# note, "running build" does not appear
```

While fiddling with the build and test commands this way may be a bit silly, the issue exists with other commands as well. Initially I thought this was a flaw in the flake8 command and reported this there, see https://gitlab.com/pycqa/flake8/issues/260. With flake8, the test command behaves differently/worse than with build (with which "build test" works); "flake8 test" runs flake8 but not test, and "test flake8" runs test, not flake8.

>pip and setuptools distributions, and so are fully supported.

Thus, one could bootstrap venv via `curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pypa/virtualenv/master/virtualenv.py | python - <venvname>`

issue_title: move client slide location validation to a separate module
issue_body:
Having worked with this library a bit more, it made more sense to remove the fetching and validation of locations from the main Configuration object. This could be replaced by a separate import e.g.

    from hdx.locations import get_valid_locations
    locations = get_valid_locations(configuration) #makes a network call to appropriate HDX endpoint
    if some_code not in locations:

This way the control over how network requests is made, or how the list of locations is cached, is the responsibility of the library user. If the locations is a plain python List this means it can also be replaced by a test object without patching or deep mocks

issue_title: Wrong line numbers reported in find-untranslated
issue_body:
See remarks by @vincentfretin in pull request #32.
It is basically this problem in lxml and maybe most xml libraries: https://bugs.launchpad.net/lxml/+bug/526799

Take this file, simplified from the `confirm.pt` that Vincent points to:

```
<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xml:lang="en"
      xmlns:i18n="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n"
      lang="en"
      i18n:domain="plone">
<body>
  <input
      type="submit"
      value="Confirm action"
      name="form.button.confirm"
      class="standalone" />
</body>
</html>
```

Running find-untranslated on it complains about line 3:

```
confirm.pt:3:2:
-ERROR- - value attribute of <... submit/button> lacks i18n:attribute
```

This is because lxml turns the content into:

```
<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:i18n="http://xml.zope.org/namespaces/i18n" xml:lang="en" lang="en" i18n:domain="plone">
<body>
  <input type="submit" value="Confirm action" name="form.button.confirm" class="standalone"/>
</body>
</html>
```

So every tag tag is now on one line instead of possibly multiple.

What semantics should "acknowledged event delivery" have?

An EVENT is to be answered by EVENT_RECEIVED

1. .. as soon as the event was received (that is potentially even before the user subscription handler fires)
2. .. after the user subscription handler has ended (regardless whether that user code did raise or not)
3. .. after the user subscription handler has ended - but only if it ended successfully
4. .. after the user subscription handler has ended - and the EVENT_RECEIVED contains a flag indicating the success/failure of the user subscription handler

> Depending on what we chose for above (what semantics does MQTT has?), we then might want to change the name of the new WAMP message (EVENT_DELIVERED, EVENT_PROCESSED)

issue_title: synchronous producer hangs when kafka is unreachable
issue_body:
_This issue was brought to my attention by Michael Kowalchik in the [mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pykafka-user/iyZlHj_NTDU)_

**Steps to reproduce**
- Run a synchronous producer pointing at a kafka cluster with a single node
- Kill the single kafka node while the producer is running
- Observe that the producer's main thread hangs and must be killed externally

The expected result is that the producer's main thread should raise a `SocketDisconnectedError` from `produce()`.

The following sequence of events causes this bug. When a broker is unavailable, `SocketDisconnectedError` is raised and caught. If there are no brokers available, [this call to _update()](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2.1.2/pykafka/producer.py#L366) raises an [exception](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2.1.2/pykafka/cluster.py#L286). This in turn [kills the worker thread](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2.1.2/pykafka/producer.py#L443), meaning that code after the `_update` call, including pushing the original `SocketDisconnectedError` onto the delivery reports queue, does not execute. On the main thread, `produce()` is still waiting for the [delivery report](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2.1.2/pykafka/producer.py#L268), which will never come because the worker has died.

Possible solutions include ignoring certain exception types from the `_update()` call, or catching any exception that happens between `to_retry` being populated and the delivery report being enqueued. I'd lean toward simply catching and warning about [this specific exception](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/2.1.2/pykafka/cluster.py#L286), maybe giving it a more specific name in the process.

Upon further inspection, it looks like the lein command generated is missing the single quotes with the new argument, the generated command is below:

```
lein run -m streamparse.commands.run/-main topologies/wordcount.clj -t 0 --option 'topology.workers=2' --option 'topology.acker.executors=2' --option 'streamparse.log.path="/my/user/path/wordcount/logs"' --option 'streamparse.log.level="debug"' --option my.conf="TEST"
```

Related to #50 

The correct combination of these two should be as follows:

|--verbose  |  --silent | desired behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
|absent (default is False) | absent (default is False) | all messages except those printed by info |
|absent (default is False) | present (True) | no messages printed
| present (True) | absent (default is False) | all messages, including info are printed
| present (True) |  present (True) | error |

Current behavior is:

|--verbose  |  --silent | current behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
|absent (default is False) | absent (default is False) | all messages except those printed by info |
|absent (default is False) | present (ignored, so the default - False) | all messages except those printed by info |
| present (True) | absent (default is False) | all messages, including info are printed
| present (True) |  present (ignored, so the default - False) | all messages, including info are printed |
It doesn't give much of any info now.

issue_title: UTF-8 character incorrectly interpreted as OSC
issue_body:
When trying to upgrade my project to use the current git master, I needed to send data as bytes.
This works, however I can't decode UTF-8 data. For example, the following test script will fail:
```
import pyte
screen = pyte.Screen(10, 1)
stream = pyte.Stream(screen)
c = b"\xE2\x80\x9D" #u+201d
stream.feed(c)
print(screen.display)
```
The first character of the screen should be '”', but is blank.

It looks like pyte is interpreting the 9d as OSC, causing problems from there.
According to Wikipedia articles on [ANSI escape codes][1] and [C0 and C1 control codes][2], in UTF-8 mode, OSC (also ST, CSI) should be encoded in UTF-8, so in this case 0xc2 0x9d.

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#Sequence_elements
[2]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C0_and_C1_control_codes

issue_title: Question: Should there be a Client Credentials Authorizer?
issue_body:
I was working on an example of the Client Credentials grant when I realized that this is a possibility.

The `RefreshTokenAuthorizer` tracks expiration time and automatically requests new tokens for the user.
However, there are no automated utilities for managing tokens fetched via Client Credentials grants.

It occurred to me that we could define a `ClientCredentialsAuthorizer` built from a `ConfidentialAppAuthClient` and a set of scopes.
The scopes are necessary in order to specify what resource server (and scopes against it) are used -- the authorizer can fetch and store access tokens via the client credentials grant, always choosing the top-level token.
We could even have it error-out if `other_tokens` are returned (i.e. bad scopes supplied).

Does this have value? Would it help client authors?

issue_title: Document usage of the SDK against Preview
issue_body:
This runs much deeper than #236.
We need to document usage, including, but not limited to:
- Preview client registration
- Preview identities/endpoints/entities being distinct from their Production counterparts, and the absence of any magic or automatic mirroring
- Setting up recommended patterns for doing environment dispatch to get at Preview (i.e. you might want a single codebase, but multiple client IDs wrapped in an env var check)

The current Getting Started docs still refer users to tokens.globus.org, even though that method of loading tokens is slated for removal before v1.
I think the preferred method for developers playing with the SDK to get tokens should be some form of client registration (as opposed to the CLI).

Keeping this low friction is going to be hard.
Perhaps we need to support client credentials in the config, and do some implicit token fetching.
That can be future work. Just having a quick snippet that does client credentials grants which we tell people to include isn't so bad for the interim.

Notably, if we make people register clients, the ConfidentialAppAuthClient class handles some of this. The real trick is getting a working Authorizer for TransferClients.

issue_title: Support a TensorBuilder patch for TFLearn
issue_body:
Hi,
>[TensorBuilder](https://github.com/cgarciae/tensorbuilder) is light-weight extensible library that enables you to easily create complex deep neural networks using functions from any Tensor-based library through a functional fluent immutable API based on the Builder Pattern. As a side effect, TensorBuilder is a library that gives expressive power to any Tensor-based library that decide to implement a TensorBuilder patch.

TensorBuilder hosts a patch for `tflearn`, using this patch you can rewrite the code from the Getting Started

```python
mport tflearn

net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 784])
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 64)
net = tflearn.dropout(net, 0.5)
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 10, activation='softmax')
net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy')

model = tflearn.DNN(net)
```
like this
```python
mport tflearn
import tensorbuilder as tb
import tensorbuilder.patches.tflearn.patch

model = (
    tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, 784]).builder()
    .fully_connected(64)
    .dropout(0.5)
    .fully_connected(10, activation='softmax')
    .regression(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy')
    .map(tflearn.DNN)
    .tensor()
)
```
A few lines of code are needed so the `tflearn` patch is at par with the `tensorflow` patch, which includes the `fully_connected` method for the`BuilderTree` class that enables you to connect a whole tree to a single output layer, useful for branched topologies. See this example of branching

```python
h = (
    x.builder()
    .fully_connected(10)
    .branch(lambda root:
    [
        root
        .fully_connected(3, activation_fn=tf.nn.relu)
    ,
        root
        .fully_connected(9, activation_fn=tf.nn.tanh)
        .branch(lambda root2:
        [
          root2
          .fully_connected(6, activation_fn=tf.nn.sigmoid)
        ,
          root2
          .map(tf.nn.dropout, keep_prob)
          .fully_connected(8, tf.nn.softmax)
        ])
    ])
    .fully_connected(6, activation_fn=tf.nn.sigmoid) #<== THIS METHOD IS MISSING
    .tensor()
)
```
I'd like to invite you to eventually host the TFLearn patch for TensorBuilder on the TFLearn library, because TensorBuilder really is just an empty shell, it doesn't even know about tensors, its a sort of functional tool for making non-composable/fluent APIs into composable/fluent APIs (in e.g. Elixir or Elm you wouldn't even need it).

Currently the use of RNNs in tflearn is quite limited by the fact that they can only operate on fixed length sequences. This limits real world applicability quite a bit since a lot of real world data doesn't have a fixed length, or has a length that exceeds the number of steps that can be unrolled on a normal GPU. 

Also, when training a network on a continuous sequence like in the shakespeare example this makes the training a lot slower and the training set a lot bigger than it needs to be since overlapping sequences need to be fed into the network on every step, instead of just continuing from the state where the network was at before. Of course the also severely limits the attention span of the RNN.

I realize that until a short while ago this was a limitation of tensorflow itself, but they recently implemented support for dynamic RNNs and the option to iterate over variable length data. More info can be found here: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/208

I would really appreciate this feature since tflearn has been the first machine learning library I've actually been able to wrap my head around and easily get good results with.

    network = tflearn.fully_connected(network, 1, activation='linear')

issue_title: Raise ValueError when appropriate if input exceeds ULONG_MAX
issue_body: Microsoft APIs like CryptStringToBinary and structs like SecBuffer and SEC_WINNT_AUTH_IDENTITY expect string length as ULONG. Python string length is defined as Py_ssize_t (ssize_t on platforms that define it). We should raise ValueError if the length of input strings exceeds ULONG_MAX. Currently these strings are truncated on 64 bit systems. Raising an explicit exception will make debugging much easier than authentication failing.

issue_title: api: add optional headers dict to put_object, fput_object, and copy_object
issue_body:
Many tools support S3 storage classes.  HashBackup does.  To do that, a header needs to be included in put and copy requests:
```
headers = {'x-amz-storage-class': 'STANDARD_IA'}
```
This can't really be done in the constructor when creating the client, because within a bucket, files can be in different storage classes.  When uploading small files less than 52K, Infrequent Access is actually more expensive than Standard storage because for IA, Amazon always charges for at least 128K.

I can take a look at doing this if it would be acceptable.

The change would be:
- add optional headers dict to put/fput/copy object apis, default is {}
- for small objects, pass the header dict to _do_put_object
-- in _do_put_object, add the Content-Length, Content-Type, and Content-Md5 headers to the header dict instead of creating a new dict
- for large objects, pass headers to _do_put_multipart_object; the headers go in the Initiate Multipart Upload request.  Looks a little messier, but same concept
- copy_object already has a header-like argument, "conditions".  It's just a class over a header dictionary.

Looks like 2 ways to do this:

1. add some new methods to CopyConditions, like set_storage_class and include a conditions arg in put_object and fput_object, like copy_object
-or-
2. add header arg to all 3.  This would be a little more flexible, because any header could be added, not just storage class.  And storage class is not exactly a "copy condition", even though very similar.

After looking at all the different header options for a put (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/RESTObjectPUT.html), I think it makes more sense to add the optional header argument to all 3 apis instead of using copyconditions.  Adding headers would enable:
- permissions
- ACLs
- storage class
- user-defined meta data
- cache controls
- tags
- server-side encryption

issue_title: Refactor CSS to use a CSS in JS library
issue_body:
Before we export our React components into their own NPM package, we should discuss how we will approach packaging our styles. One suggestion is to refactor our CSS to use a CSS in JS library. Here's a starting point - let's edit this list together based on what you all think:

### Libraries
- [Aphrodite](https://github.com/Khan/aphrodite)
- [styled-components](https://github.com/styled-components/styled-components)

### Features we need
- Theming support
- Keyframes for animations
- Pseudo-selectors like :hover
- Approachable developer experience

Theming support is probably what's most important to us right now. If we go the Aphrodite route, we need to discuss if we want to package our themes with the React components. Otherwise, we would only package the default theme and let the Electron app be the implementation of our components + themes. [Here's a gist of how to do themes in Aphrodite that explains some of the tradeoffs](https://gist.github.com/peggyrayzis/4dd15c0885540f856eccd50ab1c90f28). [react-with-styles](https://github.com/airbnb/react-with-styles) is a possibility here.

I believe [styled-components supports both options](https://github.com/styled-components/styled-components/blob/master/docs/theming.md), but I need to look more into it since I've never worked with it before. Looks promising though.

Couple things I'd like to see in a later refactor:

* Rely on `Object.freeze` frozen objects instead of Immutable.JS
  * Less dependencies
  * Would require refactors in our reducers, commutable, main app
* Instead of having separate transforms and displayOrder objects, lump them into a simple `Registry` or something else that provides access to the transforms and displayOrder, allows registration

Definitely a wish list item. Since I was thinking about it, figured it would be better for me to document it here and keep working on publishing with lerna.

issue_title: Demoted node does not start on fail over when using synchronous mode
issue_body:
I am testing 3 nodes in a cluster with  synchronous mode.

```
patronictl -c /etc/patroni/baruwa.yml list baruwa
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
| Cluster | Member                       | Host         |  Role  |  State  | Lag in MB |
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
| baruwa  | db.home.topdog-software.com  | 192.168.1.15 | Leader | running |       0.0 |
| baruwa  | db2.home.topdog-software.com | 192.168.1.33 |        | running |       0.0 |
| baruwa  | db3.home.topdog-software.com | 192.168.1.34 |        | running |       0.0 |
+---------+------------------------------+--------------+--------+---------+-----------+
```

When i trigger a manual failover using `patronictl -c /etc/patroni/baruwa.yml failover baruwa` The fail over happens and a different host takes over as the leader. However the previous leader is not able to startup postgresql as the `synchronous_standby_names` option is not removed from `postgresql.conf` file.

If i stop the patroni process and start it up again, the `postgresql.conf` file is updated removing the `synchronous_standby_names` option allowing postgresql to startup and rejoin the cluster.
Would be useful to have the TLS fix as an official PyPI release.

issue_title: [Partially Fixed] Row index gets lost when transferring Python DataFrame to R

issue_title: index.html#documentation does not switch to documentation tab
issue_body:
The documentation pages have a "Home" button that links to http://vatlab.github.io/SOS/index.html#documentation, which however does not switch to the documentation tab.

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/12131273/twitter-bootstrap-tabs-url-doesnt-change
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7862233/twitter-bootstrap-tabs-go-to-specific-tab-on-page-reload-or-hyperlink

might have a solution for this.

#488 has been implemented but it is up to the user to add the restrictions to their `.hosts.yml` file. It is possible to allow system admin to configure the server and enforce certain restrictions. For example, in `/etc/sos.yml`,

```
server_ip:
    process:
        max_mem: 4G
    PBS:
        max_mem: 64G
```

sos could check such file before the task is started.

Note that system admin can enforce the task restrictions manually, by stating `max_mem` etc as policy, and check the tasks with commands such as `sos status task_id -v4 | grep max_mem`.

issue_title: Showing multiple DAGs at the same time.
issue_body:
RIght now when we have multiple DAGs (e.g. one master one nested), only one DAG is displayed so the animation would switch between DAGs. It would be good to display them side by side or up and down so that we can see all steps in one figure.

It would be even better if we can use subgraphs to connect them, something like

```
[default] ===> [nested ]
```

issue_title: Control of max submitted jobs to host
issue_body:
I got this error when submitting jobs with SoS to our cluster:

```
sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Job violates accounting/QOS policy (job submit limit, user's size and/or time limits)
```

I then checked: `showq` indicates I have submitted 482 jobs (!! most of them declined, of course) . But in my configuration file I did have:

```
    max_running_jobs: 30
```

also I have set `-J` to 30. Am I doing something wrong here? It is true that `-J` will override `max_running_jobs`, and SoS should at least recognize one of these settings?

issue_title: Docker image not properly shutdown by keyboard `ctrl-c`
issue_body:
Here is the MWE:

```
[1]
run: docker_image='gaow/stress'
  stress --cpu 6 --timeout 3m
```
   
Here is a screenshot of what I'm getting:

![2018-04-23-12-00-14_scrot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/917985/39141636-f6c008a4-46ed-11e8-95e5-f932a6ccddf3.png)

You see I've terminated the run but there is still `stress` instances. I had to additionally `killall stress` to end them all.

Right around the time that I stumbled upon dask-searchcv, scikit-learn 0.19 was released where pipelines now support the memory parameter:
http://scikit-learn.org/stable/whats_new.html#version-0-19

As such, perhaps this section of the docs should be revised:
> With the regular scikit-learn version, each stage of the pipeline must be fit for each of the combinations of the parameters, even if that step isn’t being searched over.

I'd be interested to hear if any work was done to compare/contrast dask-searchcv vs scikit-learn's changes in 0.19. Presumably dask-searchcv allows you to more easily harness multiple machines, but perhaps some rudimentary benchmarks could/would be interesting/appropriate.

From:
http://dask-ml.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hyper-parameter-search.html#efficient-search

issue_title: rebuild_yara_rule method does not correctly quote metadata fields
issue_body:
Hey,

In this part of the rebuild yara method there is no accounting for the data type present in the value of each metadata key which means resulting rules have metadata 
```
# Rule Metadata
        if rule.get('metadata'):
            unpacked_meta = [u'\n\t\t{key} = {value}'.format(key=k, value=v)
                             for k, v in rule['metadata'].items()]
            rule_meta = u'\n\tmeta:{}\n'.format(u''.join(unpacked_meta))
        else:
            rule_meta = u''
```

A lazy fix for this would be to alter it so that we assume every value in the metadata is a string:

```
if rule.get('metadata'):
            unpacked_meta = [u'\n\t\t{key} = "{value}"'.format(key=k, value=v)
                             for k, v in rule['metadata'].items()]
            rule_meta = u'\n\tmeta:{}\n'.format(u''.join(unpacked_meta))
        else:
            rule_meta = u''
```

There might be a cleverer way of figuring it out though, since there are probably only 3 cases (int,str,list) that are ever used.

Cheers,
Tom

issue_title: F200 and SR300 odd formatting in serial numbers
issue_body:
Using Xubuntu with 4.4 kernel.

I'm seeing some weird device ids coming out of librealsense containing lots of "FF", also on one camera (SR300) there is a space character at the end of the serial number.

Querying the serial number directly with udevadm like so yields more sane serial numbers.

    udevadm info --query=all --name=/dev/video2 | grep -oP 'ID_SERIAL_SHORT=\K([0-9]+)'

| Source | Serial number |
|---|---|
| udevadm | 039140052101 |
| librealsense | 03FFFFFF9140052101 |

What gives? Which is correct? Can you please avoid whitespace in serial numbers? Please advice.

Camera Model: F200 
Firmware version: 2.60.0.0
Operating System & Version: Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS
Kernel Version (Linux Only): 4.4.0-45-generic
Build System: Makefile

Not strictly an issue, more of a question.

Do you consider adding timeouts for the rs_wait_for_frames call? I see it has some std::chrono fixed 5 second timeout, but this is just busy looping in infinite while loops, not a blocking call.

It is not passed to struct timeval for the select in v4l2 backend or libusb transfer setups - libuvc also hardcodes 5 second timeout.

This is perhaps not that critical as proper blocking is not that useful for cameras that don't have external triggers (like dc1394 or PointGrey), but I think it would be useful to have user settable timeout anyway (5 s might be too long).

In the meantime I'll read the code some more and think how to approach this issue.

### Issue Description
<Describe your issue / question / feature request / etc..>
I used code below to show depth of the red dot, are there any problems in the code? I find the value inormally turns to 0 meter sometimes. Thank you very much?
import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
pipeline = rs.pipeline()
config = rs.config()
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 640, 360, rs.format.z16, 30)
config.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 640, 480, rs.format.bgr8, 30)
profile = pipeline.start(config)
align_to = rs.stream.color
align = rs.align(align_to)
try:
    while True:
        frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
        aligned_frames = align.process(frames)
        aligned_depth_frame = aligned_frames.get_depth_frame() # aligned_depth_frame is a 640x480 depth image
        color_frame = aligned_frames.get_color_frame()
        if not aligned_depth_frame or not color_frame:
            continue
        depth_image = np.asanyarray(aligned_depth_frame.get_data())
        depth = depth_image.astype(float)
        depth_scale = profile.get_device().first_depth_sensor().get_depth_scale()
        depth = depth * depth_scale
       
        color_image = np.asanyarray(color_frame.get_data())
        cv2.circle(color_image, (320,240),3,(0,0,255),5)
        cv2.putText(color_image, str(depth[320,240],5)+'m' , (330,230), cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_TRIPLEX, 0.5, (0, 0, 255), 1,False)
        depth_colormap = cv2.applyColorMap(cv2.convertScaleAbs(depth_image, alpha=0.03), cv2.COLORMAP_JET)
        images = np.hstack((color_image, depth_colormap))
        cv2.namedWindow('Align Example', cv2.WINDOW_AUTOSIZE)
        cv2.imshow('Align Example', images)
        cv2.waitKey(1)
finally:
    pipeline.stop()

issue_title: Frames didn't arrive error - after improper shutdown
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | D435 | 
| Firmware Version | 05.08.15.00 | 
| Operating System & Version | Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.13.0-32-generic |
| SDK Version |  2.9.1 |

Hi all,

I am working on a project where I am using a realsense D435 sensor to get data about the position of some objects and process that using OpenCV.

Normally, the sensor works fine. But if the power is turned off to the computer (or the computer is reset) while the program is running, when I try to run the program after powerup, I get:
```
RealSense error calling rs2_pipeline_wait_for_frames(pipe:0x8ceea0):
Frame didn't arrived within 5000
```
The device is detected by query_devices() and I can read information from it using dev.get_info() but when I try to stream the depth data I get the error.

Plugging the cable out and then in again solves the issue. Just shutting down the computer, waiting a few minutes and powering up again, does not solve it.

Unfortunately, most of the shutdowns that I can expect will be improper ones (power failures) and plugging the cable out is not an option because the sensor is mounted on an automatic crane and it could takes hours until a technician gets to the power cabinet and re-plugs the cable.

When I try to run realsense-viewer, the device is detected, but when I try to stream the depth data it also fails:
```
 26/01 19:59:28,850 WARNING [140202273707776] (sensor.cpp:313) Unregistered Media formats : [ UYVY ]; Supported: [ ]
 26/01 19:59:28,852 WARNING [140202273707776] (backend-v4l2.cpp:1100) Pixel format 36315752-1a66-a242-9065-d01814a likely requires patch for fourcc code RW16!
 26/01 19:59:28,852 WARNING [140202273707776] (sensor.cpp:313) Unregistered Media formats : [ RW16 ]; Supported: [ ]
 26/01 19:59:44,757 WARNING [140202240136960] (backend-v4l2.cpp:809) Empty frame has arrived.
 (empty frame has arrived repeats 31 times)
 26/01 19:59:49,776 WARNING [140202240136960] (backend-v4l2.cpp:827) Frames didn't arrived within 5 seconds
 26/01 19:59:49,787 WARNING [140202160944896] (backend-v4l2.cpp:827) Frames didn't arrived within 5 seconds
 26/01 19:59:54,781 WARNING [140202240136960] (backend-v4l2.cpp:827) Frames didn't arrived within 5 seconds
 26/01 19:59:54,792 WARNING [140202160944896] (backend-v4l2.cpp:827) Frames didn't arrived within 5 seconds
...
```
The `udevadm monitor` does not show anything when I run my program or realsense-viewer.

I have tried to use linux commands to replicate plugging the usb out and in again (like the ones presented here: https://askubuntu.com/questions/342061/power-on-off-usb-ports), but without any sucess.

So what should I try next?

Hi,

I'm able to generate a PLY from my D415 but I need to get an organized point cloud such as a PCD, PTX or E57.
Is there a way to do it directly with th SDK 2.0 or PCL library?

Thanks in advance


| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D400                                                             | 
| Operating System & Version |   Linux (Ubuntu 16.04)                        | 
| Platform                                 | PC                                                            |
| SDK Version                            |             2.0                                          |

We've received numerous requests for S3 event support, but we never had a tracking issue for it.  As we start to flesh out sources for lambda events, I wanted to make sure we have a consistent approach.

I propose adding an `on_s3_event()` decorator, and that all future event sources use the `on_<service>_<something else>` naming convention.  The input parameter is a small abstraction over the event payload to support the most common use case: downloading the object from S3.

Because we don't yet support managed resources (https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/516), I initially wanted this first version to support pre-existing S3 buckets.  That is, you tell us what existing bucket you want us to configure, and we'll configure the appropriate s3 bucket notifications for you (along with the needed policy updates).  Here's a complete example of the famous s3 image resizer:

```python
from chalice import Chalice

import boto3

app = Chalice(app_name='s3resize')
s3 = boto3.client('s3')


@app.on_s3_event(bucket='my-preexisting-bucket')
def resize_image(event):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w') as f:
        s3_client.download_file(event.bucket, event.key, f.name)
        resized_file = f.name + '.thumbnail'
        with open(Image.open(f.name)) as image:
            image.thumbnail(tuple(x / 2 for x in image.size))
            image.save(resized_file)
        s3_client.upload_file(filename=resized_file,
                              bucket='my-preexisting-dest-bucket',
                              key='%s-thumbnail' % event.key)
```

That's the entire thing.  You run `chalice deploy`, and assuming the source and dest bucket exists, everything just works.

A few things to note in this example:

* There's an optional `filters` and `events` kwarg in the decorator.  The default `events` is `s3:ObjectCreated:*` so it's omitted from the example.
* The `event` param has a `.bucket` and `.key` attribute to make it easy to download objects from s3.  Note this is assuming a single record is sent in the event payload.

## Concerns

The biggest concern is that due to us not managing the S3 bucket for you, you have to use a pre-existing bucket, which means that the **SAM template generation won't work**.  The notification configuration is a property of an `AWS::S3::Bucket` which means you *have* to create the S3 bucket as part of your CFN template.  I see a few possible options here:

1. Just don't support `on_s3_event` in SAM templates, generate a helpful error message.  This feature would only be available via `chalice deploy`.
2. "Make it work" by using custom CFN resources.  I haven't done a whole lot of research on this, other than to confirm that this is a viable approach.  I'd need to think this through more.  It also feels a bit odd leaving a lambda function/custom resource lying around.  This has a lot of risk and uncertainty, so it would probably delay this feature being released.
3. Add preliminary support for resources (#516).  We'd start with an S3 bucket, and we'll need to do another pass through the original proposal.  You could then say something like:


```python

@app.resource('s3.Bucket')
class MyBucket:
    name = 'some-bucket-name'

@app.on_s3_event(bucket=MyBucket)
def resize_image(event): pass
```

In this example, we'd also create the S3 bucket for you.  Even if we did option (3), we'd still need to decide whether or not to support `chalice deploy` working with pre-existing S3 buckets.  The only benefit of (3) is that it gives people a path forward that rely on the `chalice package` command, you just have to be ok with us managing the S3 bucket for you.

FWIW I have most of this implemented.  We just need to decide on which of the above options we want to go with for `chalice package`.  Let me know what you all think.

cc @stealthycoin @kyleknap

issue_title: Link_df runs very slow with large data sets
issue_body:
I'm attempting to link particle positions to form trajectories using link_df for a data set containing ~1,700 particles for 90,000 frames.  In attempting this, I noticed link_df was running very slow.  After 12 hours, the code had completed under 8,000 frames (I estimate that the entire data set would have taken maybe 4 days).  While I understand that that is a huge data set, I previously was able to complete the linking of the same number of particles (~1,700) for 24,000 frames, which took about 4 hours.  Naively, I was expecting the time to scale linearly, and so this 90k frame data set would take 12-16 hours (much more reasonable than 4 days).  But this was not the case.

My hypothesis is there is a memory issue.  The 90k frame data file is ~3 GB, and uses 10-15% of my machine's memory (linux server, found using "top" command).  Any suggestions on how this process can be sped up?

Some useful information, versions of various packages are: python 2.7.10, numba 0.12.2, numpy 1.8.2, trackpy 0.2.4rc7, and pandas 0.14.1.

Thanks for your help.

issue_title: Consider if auth tokens should be removed from channel URLs in conda-meta JSON files
issue_body:
Assuming conda is aware of URL-embedded auth tokens (see #5750 and https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-server/issues/1304) it seems like it might be problematic to embed them into the `conda-meta/package-version.json` files for a few reasons:

1. the package cache may be a shared resource and Bob shouldn't have access to Alice's auth tokens.

2. the auth token may expire or be denied and future operations that need to know "where to get the package" should provide a set of auth tokens relevant at that point in time (which may be the same ones), not assume that auth tokens used in the past are going to be OK.

It appears that `conda create --clone` uses this information (though for reasons stated in #5953 perhaps it shouldn't!)

issue_title: Request: a command to run a command under env
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [ ] bug report
  - [x] feature request

conda environments are easy to access from shell scripts. But it's not so nice from e.g. some Python code. Ideally, a piece of code that knows the name of an environment should be able to execute easily a command inside that environment.

Let's say I want to run `pip -V` inside a given environment named "foobar". This won't work:
```python
subprocess.check_call(['conda', 'activate', 'foobar'])
subprocess.check_call(['pip', '-V'])
```

What I'm suggesting is to be able to write something like this:
```python
subprocess.check_call(['conda', 'env', 'run', '-n', 'foobar', 'pip', '-V'])
```
... making the `pip` subcommand execute with all the right environment variables set up (such as PATH, CONDA_PREFIX, etc.).

issue_title: conda unconditionally attempts to chown .conda/environments.txt even when it is not necessary
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

1. Delete `.conda/environments.txt`, confirm that `.conda` is correctly permissioned.
2. Run `conda install` with a recent Conda (I'm running 4.4.4-py27_0) from a user that does not have `chown` rights (for example, by default a user in a Docker environment will not have the ability to chown)

issue_title: "conda install" could create a "hint list" of symlinks to be used by "conda clean"
issue_body:
I just noticed that `conda clean -p` states "Warning: this does not check for symlinked packages".  

Presumably this is potentially very bad: someone who runs "conda clean -p" could blast a stack of packages that are only linked to via symlinks, and destroy the operational environment of many other people, perhaps even including their own.

Perhaps a first-step improvement on this would be for "conda install" (and, I suppose, "conda create" by implication) commands that are going to create symlinks to also have those operations somehow register a sym-link "hint" for the package so there is a two-way record that the sym-link exists.  Flat files would be the easiest way to do this, accepting that there will be file/directory permission issues, and problems if there is ever concurrent operations.

In any case, if such a "sym-link hint list" existed it would then be possible for "conda clean" to check to see if those sym-links existed (assuming a uniform file system view from the environment where "conda clean" is being executed, and anywhere else the associated conda environments are accessible).  Packages would only be cleaned if there were no valid sym-link hints.

    + .index.json
  - /wind-64

Currently zope-testrunner -jN runs the first test layer serially, and only then starts forking N subprocesses for parallel execution.  People have been creating workarounds for this by adding an empty test layer named something like `.!no tests here!` to get the 1st serial layer out of the way ASAP, so the rest of the tests can run in parallel.

The change in 4.4.5 (run the unit test layer first, https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.testrunner/+bug/497871) broke this hack.

Suggestion: if the test runner sees -j on the command line, let it create an empty test layer automatically and insert it in front of all other layers.  (Maybe make it conditional on there being at least two other layers, maybe don't bother -- who would use -j when there's only one test layer?)

issue_title: how to change the .fits file format in galsim?
issue_body:
I have generated an image with galsim, and write it to a .fits file.
the format of the .fits file is -32, float. 
What shall I do if I want to make it double precision?
Or Is there any other way to out put the image just some simple ascii file with double precision.

I have a possibly interesting WCS problem.

I have a FITS header that is in TAN-SIP format, which should be readable by WCSTools, Starlink AST, and one other library that GalSim doesn't know how to talk to.  Given the warnings in fitswcs.py about WCSTools being slow, I went with Starlink AST.  The problem I'm having is that if I do something like
```
wcs = galsim.PyAstWCS('myfile.fits')
```
it makes a WCS with no problem and I can use the various methods like ```toImage()``` and so on.  But if I do
```
wcs = galsim.FitsWCS('myfile.fits')
```
then I get this warning "All the fits WCS types failed to read myfile.fits.  Using AffineTransform instead, which will not really be correct."  As far as I can tell, when the FitsWCS constructor is looping over the WCS types, it really is hitting an exception when it does this two-step process of reading in the header and then using the ```_readHeader``` method, unlike when directly instantiating the ```PyAstWCS``` directly.

In principle this isn't a very big deal; I could just require Starlink AST for these WCSs, instead of using FitsWCS (which would allow users with either Starlink AST or WCSTools to use these WCSs).  But it seemed a bit odd so I thought I would ask if this is something you've run into before, @rmjarvis , or have some ideas about?

issue_title: Add a new general category: Statistics
issue_body:
Items in this category are the core model properties. They are displayed without being counted towards the final score.

However, tests in the other categories may act on these, i.e. by making a requirement towards a certain ratio/ percentage:
The amount of total reactions is a core property. The ratio of blocked reactions to total reactions should be as low as possible.

Not sure if this is implemented already.

Set all uptake rates to zero, and maximize all secretion rates. All should return zero, i.e. nothing is produced for free.

PS: Not sure how you guys are handling different formats for specifying uptake/secretion reactions. In BiGG and modelSEED models uptake and secretion are encoded by the same exchange reaction, but some people use different reactions for uptake and secretion (both with positive fluxes). So I'm not sure how to fully automate this process in a way that blindly guesses the correct directions.

issue_title: TabularDataset class with header does not match fields by name
issue_body:
Currently, the TabularDataset class matches each feature in a csv/tsv file to its field by requiring the (name, Field) tuples passed to the fields kwarg to be in the same order as the columns csv/tsv file.

I think this is counterintuitive, especially if the csv/tsv file has a header. If there is a header, I think that the user should be allowed to specify the fields similar to the json format, where they pass in a dict mapping the column name to the (name, Field) tuple.

Here’s an example of what I’m thinking.

```
$ head -n 2 train.csv
"text","label"
"hello",0
```

```python
>>> pos = data.TabularDataset(
...    path='train.tsv', format='csv',
...    fields={'labels': ('labels', data.Field()),
...                'text': ('text', data.Field())}
...
```

I think they should also be allowed to select a subset of the rows using this method, similar to the API for the json format (columns not in the keys will be ignored).

This should provide a more consistent and intuitive API. If the maintainers are willing to accept this API, I would be happy to implement it and send a PR.

issue_title: Dataset target datatypes vary between Datasets
issue_body:
For example, when using `torchvision.datasets.MNIST`, the targets are returned as type `int`:
```python
>>> torchvision.datasets.MNIST('./data', train=True)
<torchvision.datasets.mnist.MNIST object at 0x7feb5af89860>
>>> ds = torchvision.datasets.MNIST('./data', train=True)
>>> ds[0]
(<PIL.Image.Image image mode=L size=28x28 at 0x7FEB5AF89C18>, 5)
>>> type(ds[0][1])
<class 'int'>
```

But when using the SVHN dataset, they are returned as `numpy.int64`:

```python
>>> ds = torchvision.datasets.SVHN('./data', split='train')
>>> ds[0]
(<PIL.Image.Image image mode=RGB size=32x32 at 0x7FEB5AF89C18>, 1)
>>> type(ds[0][1])
<class 'numpy.int64'> 
```

A minor issue, I'm sure, but one nevertheless. This is because it's possible to turn a list of `int` into `torch.LongTensor`, but not `numpy.int64`:

```python
>>> torch.LongTensor([1,2,3])

 1
 2
 3
[torch.LongTensor of size 3]

>>> torch.LongTensor([np.int64(1),np.int64(2)])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
RuntimeError: tried to construct a tensor from a int sequence, but found an item of type numpy.int64 at index (0)

```

@data_ingredient.config

Hi I had a problem with getting empty pages surfing to https websites using the phantomjs.
I found that this is due to ssl-errors which can be ignored in phantomjs by setting the --ignore-ssl-errors=true flag. This however can not be set when creating a phantomjs instance via browser('phantomjs'). I build a workaround by adding the following (in bold) to phantomjs.py

 def \__init__(self, user_agent=None, load_images=True,
                 desired_capabilities=None, wait_time=2,**service_args=None**,
                 custom_headers={}, \*\*kwargs):
        capabilities = DesiredCapabilities.PHANTOMJS.copy()
        if user_agent is not None:
            capabilities['phantomjs.page.settings.userAgent'] = user_agent
        capabilities['phantomjs.page.settings.loadImages'] = load_images
        if isinstance(custom_headers, dict):
            for name, value in custom_headers.items():
                capabilities['phantomjs.page.customHeaders.%s' % name] = value
        if desired_capabilities:
            capabilities.update(desired_capabilities)
	**service_args = service_args**
        self.driver = PhantomJS(**service_args=service_args**,desired_capabilities=capabilities, **kwargs)

which passes the service args to selenium. Is there another option or is this the only way to pass service args?

issue_title: The method message_post need the subject and body be translatable
issue_body:
The method `self.message_post` need the parameter `subject` and `body` be translatable For example, the follow method raise the check `translation-required` because the parameter `subject` and `body` not is instance of `_` function ```python self.message_post( subject='Error when trying to generate the invoice', # translation-required body='

%s

%s' % ( # translation-required _('Error when trying to generate the invoice'), _('The invoice should have a datetime set.'))) ``` The correct way to this code is the follow ```python self.message_post( subject=_('Error when trying to generate the invoice'), body=_('

%s

%s' % ( _('Error when trying to generate the invoice'), _('The invoice should have a datetime set.')))) ```

issue_body:
Create test to explore the design choice described [3234](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/3234)

Possible steps to test.
 
1 . Add content to Pulp - using sync to a repoA.
2. Create a second repoB.
3. Use `add_content_units` to pass a list of urls for units to be added to repoB.
4. Assert that units are present on repoB.
5. Assert that version of repoB has changed.

Let's create a top-level directory named `scripts` and move `gen_api_docs.sh` (added by #197) and `release.sh` into it. This directory can also serve as the home for any future scripts we might want to add.

issue_title: Lookup SecurityGroups by the tag "Name" rather than GroupName
issue_body:
Both AWS API and CloudFormation allow to refer to a security group by its name if the operation runs in EC2 Classic or the default VPC. Unfortunately it uses the `GroupName` attribute that is automatically generated by AWS if the SG is a part of CloudFormation stack.

It would be a good idea to extend Senza to lookup SG during the CF template generation phase and to use the _tag_ `Name` instead. The tag can be set by another ("system") Senza stack definition, thus allowing the cross-stack references.

Another option would be to support the new cross-stack references that are recently introduced by Amazon:  https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/aws-cloudformation-update-yaml-cross-stack-references-simplified-substitution/

issue_title: DateTime.TryParse produces different results in different environments
issue_body:
# DateTime.TryParse produces different results in different environments



# General
I am trying to parse a date, with DateTime.TryParse and with a provided Norwegian culture and no DatetimeStyles:
``` csharp
var result: DateTime.TryParse("29.01", new CultureInfo("nb-NO", false), DateTimeStyles.None, out var parsedDateTime)
``` 

I am purposely sending in "29.01" and expecting the result to parse to a default Datetime: "29.01.2018",
I also expect that 29.01.18 and 29.01.2018 parses to the correct dates. 

In norway the date format is dd.MM.YYYY HH:mm
Code:
https://github.com/Doff3n/HelloDate

Commands:
- dotnet build
- dotnet run

Running this on my PC:
- dotnet run
parsedDateTime: 29.01.2018 00:00:00
result: True

Running this on the VM:
- dotnet run
parsedDateTime: 01.01.0001 00.00.00
result: False

issue_title: yield from syntax error in _test_asyncio.py for 15.1.0
issue_body:
Hi!

I've downloaded the pyzmq-15.1.0.tar.gz source code and am building an RPM package for RHEL 7.x x86_64.  RHEL 7 ships with /usr/bin/python as python 2.7.5 (plus "vendor sauce" patches and backports).  The organization I work for is a strong proponent of OS packages for all add-on modules for scripting languages like Python, Perl, and Ruby, so we don't use tools like pip, cpan, or rubygem to directly install add-on modules.

The standard RPM machinery on RHEL has a process that runs to re-compile the python byte code automatically just before the binary RPM package is generated.  This script is called

```
    /usr/lib/rpm/brp-python-bytecompile
```

When that process runs over all the files in the build-root (a temporary location created as a staging area to collect all the files that should be part of the package) for pyzmq, it errors out with the following:

```
Bytecompiling .py files below /var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager/usr/lib64/python2.7 using /usr/bin/python2.7
Compiling /var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/zmq/tests/_test_asyncio.py ...
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/zmq/tests/_test_asyncio.py", line 37
    yield from a.send(b'hi')
             ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

I'm not an expert Pythoneer, but based on my understanding, it appears that "yield from" was formalized as part of PEP-0380 and is essentially new syntax for Python 3.3 and later.  Am I correct in thinking that's why the python 2.7.5 from RHEL 7 is failing to (re)byte-compile that file?

Since this is in the tests subdirectory, I'm planning on working around the issue by removing that directory before issuing the

```
  python setup.py install --root=/var/tmp/python-pyzmq-15.1.0-1.packager
```

directive in the RPM spec file. See any problem with that approach?

Hello. ### Brief I guess `` tag shall use nc prefix, because "nc:edit-config" "nc:target" all have "nc" prefix, but "config" doesn't. You had an issue(https://github.com/ncclient/ncclient/issues/56) about it, but there wasn't any logs attached. ### Details I try to send edit-config message to sysrepo(latest version) using ncclient(0.6).
Client code

```python #! /usr/bin/env python import sys, os, warnings warnings.simplefilter("ignore", DeprecationWarning) from ncclient import manager def nc_edit_config(con): xml = ''' eth0 false ''' ret = con.edit_config(target='candidate', config=xml) print("Success? {}". format(ret.ok)) def main(): with manager.connect(host='localhost', port=830, username='netconf', password='netconf', hostkey_verify=False) as nc_con: nc_edit_config(nc_con) if __name__== "__main__": main() ```

Client output

``` > python ncclient_err_example.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "ncclient_err_example.py", line 27, in main() File "ncclient_err_example.py", line 24, in main nc_edit_config(nc_con) File "ncclient_err_example.py", line 18, in nc_edit_config ret = con.edit_config(target='candidate', config=xml) File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ncclient/manager.py", line 170, in wrapper return self.execute(op_cls, *args, **kwds) File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ncclient/manager.py", line 240, in execute raise_mode=self._raise_mode).request(*args, **kwds) File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ncclient/operations/edit.py", line 67, in request return self._request(node) File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ncclient/operations/rpc.py", line 336, in _request raise self._reply.error ncclient.operations.rpc.RPCError: Missing element's "namespace". ```

#### Message from client ```xml eth0 false ``` #### Server error ``` ERROR: Missing element's "namespace". (/nc:rpc/nc:edit-config/config) ``` I am new to NETCONF, at first i've sent bugreport to sysrepo https://github.com/CESNET/libyang/issues/535. The answer was that it's ncclient issue, i think they are right. Quote from their answer: > The error says that /nc:rpc/nc:edit-config/config element does not have a namespace. Looking at the received message, you see that the config element really does not have a namespace, it would need to be nc:config to work. Without the prefix it uses the parent's namespace which is none in your case.

issue_title: 4 digit instance numbers make no sense. (manual import from sf.net)
issue_body:
Currently the instance numbers for sarra state files allow for 9999 instances of a single configuration on a single host. This is likely overkill big time. Should probably reduce to three, or perhaps evan two digits, as more than 40 instances (which would typically share an input queue) is likely to be useless. using four digit numbers gives the wrong idea (that such numbers might be sensible.)

Had started with three digit ones in the C implementation, but had to increase to four digit to match python one. Should probably change to two digits eventually.

issue_title: Moving TRiP98 related code outside pymchelper
issue_body:
Currently we have two converters which generate TRiP98-related stuff.
One is tripcube generating dose cubes and another one is tripddd generating DDD files.
Both of them require installation of many  other packages (i.e. pytrip and all its dependencies, including scipy) which makes dealing with pymchelper more complicated. Moreoever TRiP98 features may seem as additional burden for other users, i.e. interesting in MC simulation outside particle therapy.

My proposition is:
 - move tripcube into pytrip project in pytrip github group  (using pymchelper as a library)
 - move tripddd into new (called ddd?) project in pytrip github group (again using pymchelper as a library). There could also go scripts to loop over energies, generate SH12A input and nice description in README file.

I try to change a constant from model admin page save look like success, but when I refresh modify constant don't work 
### Steps to reproduce

1. `pip install django-picklefield`
2. config like as follow:
```

    'WEB_VERSION_PATCH': (0, "Show patch version for web user"),
    'TOOLS_API_URL': ("sssss", "which domain for citools api call ", 'url_field')
}

CONSTANCE_CONFIG_FIELDSETS = OrderedDict((
    ('GENERATE', [v for v in CONSTANCE_CONFIG.keys() if v.split('_')[0] not in ["WEB", "SERVICE", "TOOLS"]]),
    ('Web Frontend Options', [v for v in CONSTANCE_CONFIG.keys() if v.startswith("WEB_")]),
    ('Web Service Options', [v for v in CONSTANCE_CONFIG.keys() if v.startswith("SERVICE_")]),
    # ('CITool Fetch Options', [v for v in CONSTANCE_CONFIG.keys() if v.startswith("TOOLS_")])
))

INSTALLED_APPS=[
                ...
                'constance',
                ...
]
```
3. `./manager.py runserver`
4. modifiy on admin page 
5. don't work
       
### System configuration

* Django version: (1.10.8)
* Python version: 3.5.2

### Describe the problem

When you change one of the constance fields at the admin page it will dispatch the signal for every field, no matter if was changed or not.

It should only send the signal when an actual change was made on a field.

I took a look into https://github.com/jazzband/django-constance/blob/03704ee4f7cc70b914b1305d1e1f21b0138ddd77/constance/admin.py#L110 and the `save` method goes through all the fields without checking if it was changed.

I added a check, but I had a problem checking datetime objects, because when I get the object from the form is a datetime object and when I get the object from the `config` object it returns a string.

The comparison also affects the `modified` status since we are comparing a string and a datetime objects.

### Steps to reproduce

Use datetime objects at `CONSTANCE_CONFIG`:
```
    (
        'DISABLE_ORDERS_SINCE',
        (
            timezone(TIME_ZONE).localize(datetime(2015, 12, 24, 20)),
            'redacted',
            datetime,
        )
    ),
    (
        'DISABLE_ORDERS_UNTIL',
        (
            timezone(TIME_ZONE).localize(datetime(2016, 1, 2, 11)),
            'redacted',
            datetime,
        )
    ),
    (
        'STORE_OPENS_AT',
        ('12:00:00', 'redacted', time)
    ),
```

### System configuration

* Django version: 1.8, 1.9, 1.10
* Python version: 2.7, 3.5, pypy

### Possible solution

I did test:
```
--- a/constance/admin.py
+++ b/constance/admin.py
@@ -108,8 +108,7 @@ class ConstanceForm(forms.Form):

     def save(self):
         for name in settings.CONFIG:
-            if str(getattr(config, name)) != str(self.cleaned_data[name]):
-                setattr(config, name, self.cleaned_data[name])
+            setattr(config, name, self.cleaned_data[name])

     def clean_version(self):
         value = self.cleaned_data['version']
@@ -151,7 +150,7 @@ class ConstanceAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
             'default': localize(default),
             'help_text': _(help_text),
             'value': localize(value),
-            'modified': str(value) != str(default),
+            'modified': value != default,
             'form_field': form[name],
         }
```

But it only works on python 3.5

The [README for 1.2.0](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/86e8f9b22c9d5957b8ff6097208de69758a3c013/README.rst) states that the requirement is Python 3.5+. 

However, the [Changelog](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md) states that the minimum is Python 3.4+.

issue_title: Incorrect Access of Dictionaries in Shared Memory
issue_body:
### System information:
- Linux Ubuntu 18.04
- Ray installed through pip3
- Ray version: 0.4.0
- Python version: 3.6.5
- Num CPUs: 12; GPUs: 1 (but GPU unused here)
- (Code shown below)

### Describe the problem
Accessing a dictionary in the object store appears to be keyed into incorrectly sometimes; particularly, it appears, on the first access after (slices of?) object store arrays are stored in member variables of a class, though I have not checked the boundaries here of where else the error occurs.

I know that I am perhaps incorrectly pulling information from the store to update a member variable, because I may be feeding it slices from the object store arrays instead of copying the data. If this is the case, I can change this later. However, even if that is an incorrect usage, the store object should definitely be returning the correct information whenever I access it, not keying into the wrong item in the dict, as I am seeing it do. The second time I key in, with the same key, it produces the correct information. This is incorrect behavior for any kind of dictionary. Always on the first try it is wrong; always on the second attempt it is correct.

The same error occurs whether I use get or wait.

My use case below is a parallelized k nearest neighbors algorithm, used on a toy space here to show what it is doing in a manageable way. The Distances class will wait until kth_neighbors is called the first time to compute them, then return the list of indeces for the kth neighbor of every object in the space. I believe if I force it to recompute every time, it will produce the incorrect result every time. The original returned values I get from get() on the remotes are correct, and get put in to my Distances.neighbors dictionary correctly but as slices which access the shared data incorrectly?

### Source code
```
# To reproduce an instance of the bug:

import numpy as np
import psutil
import ray
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.spatial as sp
from tqdm import tqdm

try: ray.init()
except: pass

class Distances:

    def __init__(self):

        # A 2D, mock embedding space with 32 points:
        self.space = np.array([                                                         
            [ 0.00, 4.00 ], [ 0.25, 4.00 ], [ 2.00, 4.00 ], [ 2.50, 4.00 ],
            [-1.50, 3.00 ], [ 2.00, 3.00 ], [ 4.00, 3.00 ], [-3.00, 2.50 ],
            [-2.00, 2.50 ], [-1.75, 2.50 ], [-1.00, 2.50 ], [-2.00, 2.00 ],
            [ 0.00, 2.00 ], [ 3.50, 2.00 ], [-1.00, 1.00 ], [ 1.75, 0.50 ],
            [-3.00, 0.00 ], [ 1.00, 0.00 ], [ 3.00, 0.00 ], [ 1.00,-0.50 ],
            [ 2.00,-0.50 ], [-3.00,-1.00 ], [-1.00,-1.00 ], [ 1.25,-1.00 ],
            [-1.00,-1.50 ], [-1.50,-2.00 ], [ 1.50,-2.00 ], [ 3.00,-2.00 ],
            [-0.50,-3.00 ], [ 0.00,-3.00 ], [-2.00,-4.00 ], [ 4.00,-4.00 ] ])

        # k neighbors to calculate: furthest, nearest, and second-nearest:
        self.make_kth_neighbors = [31, 1, 2]
        self.metric_str = "euclidean"
        self.neighbors = None


    # Convenient graphing function so you can see the data and who should be
    #   nearest, second nearest, and furthest neighbors to whom:
    def graph(self):
        plt.figure(1, figsize=(5, 5))
        p = plt.axes([.1, .1, .8, .8])
        p.scatter(self.space[:,0], self.space[:,1])

        p.set_xlim((-4.2,4.2))
        p.set_ylim((-4.2,4.2))
        p.set_title('Fixed Cluster-like 2D Test Set')
        p.grid(True)
        p.set_aspect('equal', 'datalim')

        for i, tup in enumerate(self.space):
            p.annotate(str(i), tup)

        plt.show()


    def kth_neighbors(self, k):
        if k < 0: k += len(self.space)
        assert k in self.make_kth_neighbors

        # NEIGHBOR CALCULATIONS IF HAVEN'T BEFORE:
        if self.neighbors is None:

            self.neighbors = {} # dicts keyed to the k we're told to calculate

            for k in self.make_kth_neighbors:
                self.neighbors[k] = np.empty(len(self.space), dtype=np.uint64)

            # PARALLELIZATION TO FILL IN NEIGHBORS:

            # Calculate the required neighbors for the object with the given index i:
            @ray.remote
            def neighbor_row(i, space, len_space, metric_str, make_kth):

                distances = sp.distance.cdist(
                    np.atleast_2d(space[i]),
                    space,
                    metric_str
                ).squeeze()
                ordering = np.argpartition(distances, make_kth)

                nbrs = np.empty(len(make_kth))

                for j, k in enumerate(make_kth):
                    nbrs[j] = ordering[k]

                return nbrs

            # Shove some data into the object store:
            space_id = ray.put(self.space)
            metric_str_id = ray.put(self.metric_str)
            make_kth_id = ray.put(np.array(self.make_kth_neighbors))

            # Start the remotes:
            remotes = [neighbor_row.remote(
                i,
                space_id,
                len(self.space),
                metric_str_id,
                make_kth_id
            ) for i in range(len(self.space))]

            # Get the results:
            for j, vals in enumerate(zip(*ray.get(remotes))):
                self.neighbors[self.make_kth_neighbors[j]] = np.array(vals, dtype=np.uint64)

        # Return the kth-nearest neighbor of every object in the space.
        #    Since self.neighbors is a dict keyed to k values,
        #    the same k should ALWAYS produce the same array!
        return self.neighbors[k]

d = Distances()
print(d.kth_neighbors(1)) # NOTE: without print, this one doesn't print,
print(d.kth_neighbors(1)) #    while this one does. Not sure why, but it may be related.
```
Output:
```
[ 4  2  5  5  8  3  3 11 11  4  9  9 14  5 12 20 11 15 15 23 19 25 25 20
 25 22 27 20 25 24 25 26]
[ 1  0  3  2  9  2 13  8  9  8  4  8 10  6 11 17 21 19 20 17 23 16 24 19
 22 24 23 26 29 28 28 27]
```
You should know that the first array it gives are actually the values that kth_neighbors(2) should produce; ie, the second-nearest neighbors. This appears to be what it gives even if I put in 31 as well.

issue_title: Serializing/deserializing converts integer to long in python 2
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Ubuntu 17.10
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: Source
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**:  2.7.14
- **Exact command to reproduce**:
```python
In [3]: val = 0

In [4]: type(val)
Out[4]: int

In [5]: x = ray.put(val)

In [6]: type(ray.get(x))
Out[6]: long
```

### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
Serializing an integer through `ray.put()` and then deserializing it with `ray.get()` returns a `long` instead of an integer. Note that this is not an issue in python 3 where the long type does not exist.

It seems to be an issue with serialization in Arrow.

Steps to reproduce:
Start head node: 
`scripts/start_ray.sh --num-cpus 100 --num-gpus 0 --num-workers 100 --head`
Login to the second (slave) node and start ray, pointing to the head node:
`./scripts/start_ray.sh --redis-address <headnode_ip:redis_port>`

issue_title: Actor tasks take longer to return as more tasks complete.
issue_body:
This issue is introduced by commit 227c916. 

As first seen on the A3C example, here is a code snippet that illustrates the issue.

```
import ray
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime
import pickle 
def timestamp():
    return datetime.now().timestamp()
    
ray.init(num_cpus=15)

@ray.actor
class Timer():
    def __init__(self, i):
        self.id_ = i

    def compute(self, params):
        arr = [np.random.randn(200, 200) for i in range(5)]
        return arr, timestamp(), self.id_

timers = [Timer(_) for _ in range(10)]
times = []
params = np.random.randn(200, 200)
task_list = [t.compute(params) for t in timers]
while True:
    done, task_list= ray.wait(task_list)
    _, _end, _id  = ray.get(done)[0]
    times.append(timestamp() - _end)
    params = np.random.randn(200, 200)
    if len(times) % 200 == 0:
        print("%d: Last 200 average times: %f" % (len(times) / 200, np.median(times[-200:])))
        if len(times) % 1000 == 0:
            with open("./easy_test.pkl", "wb") as f:
                pickle.dump(times, f)
    task_list.append(timers[_id].compute(params))
```
We see that the median/mean of the return time (time between task finishing on actor and driver receiving the results of the task) increases as more tasks finish.

The Y-axis is seconds and X-axis is Number of Tasks finished (in chunks of 500).

![screenshot 2017-04-11 17 12 25](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4529381/24938315/347df8bc-1eeb-11e7-8038-31ebc04c69a9.png)

Here is the same metric on a commit prior to the one linked above. Notice that there is no slowdown.

![screenshot 2017-04-11 17 12 48](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4529381/24938333/5bec9cf0-1eeb-11e7-9169-c83f53222321.png)

This bug was pointed out in #1077 by @wjaskowski.

There is a bug in these lines

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/a72084c5687a6ec83a58adacd1d8ef8530590fee/python/ray/rllib/ppo/rollout.py#L108-L131

Running 

```
python ray/python/ray/rllib/train.py --alg=PPO --env=CartPole-v0 --config='{"num_sgd_iter": 5, "num_workers": 1, "model": {"fcnet_hiddens": [10, 10]}}'
```

and printing `observation_filter.rs._n` inside of the while loop gives the following output.

```
observation_filter.rs._n =  1126
observation_filter.rs._n =  2230
observation_filter.rs._n =  4464

The number of samples that the observation filter thinks it has seen grows exponentially because the line 

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/a72084c5687a6ec83a58adacd1d8ef8530590fee/python/ray/rllib/ppo/rollout.py#L130

not only updates the observation filter with new observations, but it also double counts all previous observations.
