issue_body: If the inital creation via `from_dict(**kwargs)` does not contain a `Tag` a call of `MISPEvent.add_tag(tag) ` fails with `{AttributeError}'MISPEvent' object has no attribute 'Tag'`

issue_title: Email validation, gives an error, the custom functions requires 3 arguments 4 given..
issue_body:
When creating a SOCIAL_AUTH_EMAIL_VALIDATION_FUNCTION, the documentation says that it ["should take three arguments strategy, backend and code"](http://python-social-auth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pipeline.html#email-validation). The example project also creates a function with 3 parameters.  But this gives an error that says that the custom functions requires 3 params and 4 given. 


Upon debugging I found that line 133 in the _send_email_validation()_ in strategy.py sends 4 params when calling the custom function. 

```
    def send_email_validation(self, backend, email, partial_token=None):
        email_validation = self.setting('EMAIL_VALIDATION_FUNCTION')
        send_email = module_member(email_validation)
        code = self.storage.code.make_code(email)
        send_email(self, backend, code, partial_token)
        return code
```


removing the **_partial_token_**, hides the error. 



**Is this a valid fix, or am I doing something wrong**, if yes on the former, what should I do to contribute...:)

It looks like this patch https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/ef0a4d4aeedc04ec7d068fbbb317a72e6e182a4b/social_core/backends/oauth.py
incorrectly allows `def user_data()` to assign the value of `id_token` into `access_token`.

Here is a screenshot from django `runserver_plus`:

![Alt text](http://imgur.com/Yqy8sm8 "Optional title")

issue_title: Support IOS-XR named community sets as a reserved word during anonymization
issue_body:
IOS-XR BGP policy allows you to set a BGP community by either:
    a) directly referencing the target community in the config
         `set community 65001:1111`
    b) referencing a community-set in the config
         ` community-set BAR`
         `     65001:1111`
         ` set community BAR`

A sample configuration snippet for community-sets and use in a BGP policy is shown below:

```
community-set LOCAL-NOPREPEND
  1111:2222
end-set
!
community-set LOCAL-LASTRESORT
  1111:4444
end-set
!
community-set LOCAL-HIGHLOCPREF
  1111:1111
end-set
!
!
route-policy FOO
  if destination in BAR or destination in BAZ then
    set community LOCAL-NOPREPEND
    if destination in FOOBAR then
      set community LOCAL-LASTRESORT
    endif
    if destination in BAZBAR then
      set community LOCAL-HIGHLOCPREF
      set community LOCAL-LASTRESORT
    else
      set community LOCAL-NOPREPEND
      set community LOCAL-LASTRESORT
    endif
  else
    drop
  endif
end-policy
```
Netconan will turn that config into:

```
community-set LOCAL-NOPREPEND
  1111:2222
end-set
!
community-set LOCAL-LASTRESORT
  1111:4444
end-set
!
community-set LOCAL-HIGHLOCPREF
  1111:1111
end-set
!
!
route-policy FOO
  if destination in BAR or destination in BAZ then
    set community netconanRemoved42
    if destination in FOOBAR then
      set community netconanRemoved19
    endif
    if destination in BAZBAR then
      set community netconanRemoved20
      set community netconanRemoved21
    else
      set community netconanRemoved22
      set community netconanRemoved23
    endif
  else
    drop
  endif
end-policy
```

### Problem

The current API is a great wrapper around the REST API, but many common tasks that users want to do don't necessarily map directly to REST API calls. For example, simply running a container is [a rather convoluted process](https://gist.github.com/bfirsh/d3435b3f26033dd533cc4732639ae2ef#file-getting-started-python-md).

### Proposal

I am proposing we add a higher-level API focused on what users want to do. It should be the default, recommended way of using docker-py.

It could look something like this:

```python
>>> import docker
>>> client = docker.from_env()

>>> client.containers.run("ubuntu", "echo hello world")
'hello world\n'

>>> client.containers.run("ubuntu", "tasks/reticulate-splines", detach=True)
<Container '45e6d2de7c54'>

>>> client.containers.list()
[<Container '45e6d2de7c54'>, <Container 'db18e4f20eaa'>, ...]

>>> container = client.containers.get('45e6d2de7c54')

>>> for line in container.logs(stream=True):
...   print line
Reticulating spline 2...
Reticulating spline 3...
```

[See this sketch of a README for a more examples of how it could work.](https://github.com/bfirsh/docker-py/blob/user-api/README.md) It intentionally starts with a common thing that users want to do which is complex with the current API: running containers.

### Backwards compatibility

This is a good excuse for 2.0. ;)

The new API can be built on top of the current lower-level API. The current API can be made available to users in case they want that extra flexibility and to give them a seamless migration path (`client.api.create_container(...)` or something like that).

### See also

This is building on top of @shin-'s work in #788. See also #1049 #417.

issue_body:
If I create a simple project with a setup.py like this:

```
from setuptools import setup

The existing `ltm/ssl.py` file configures client certificates, but the problem is that the BIGIP's URI for this object is different than many others.  The object is not found at the same place as we assume (`https://192.168.1.1/mgmt/tm/ltm/ssl`) it is actually under the URL `https://192.168.1.1/mgmt/tm/ltm/profiles/client-ssl/`.  The parent URI `https://192.168.1.1/mgmt/tm/ltm/profiles/` contains many different types of profiles that can be used on the BIGIP.

I think that the way we would call this in the consumer is:
```Python
client_ssl_profile = BigIP.ltm.profiles.create_client-ssl()
client_ssl_profile.description = "New Description"
client_ssl_profile.update()

client_ssl_profiles = BigIP.ltm.profiles.get_client-ssls()
for profile in client_ssl_profiles:
    print profile.name
```

I'm seeing some confusing behaviour... let talk about whether it's a bug or not!

## Repro steps:

0. Hop into a shell and instantiate yourself a gapipy client, I'll call it `g` in this example...

1. Search for departures having a certain tour dossier associated with them... found some! Then search for departures having a certain itinerary associated with them... none found.

```
    >>> len([x for x in g.departures.filter(**{'tour_dossier.id': '22758'}).all()])
    280

    >>> len([x for x in g.departures.filter(**{'structured_itineraries.variation_id': 4364}).all()])
    0
```

2. Ok, kill that shell. Start a fresh shell and we'll swap the order... search for the itinerary association first and it returns results! Search for the tour_dossier association second and it returns no results. The opposite of last time.

```
    >>> len([x for x in g.departures.filter(**{'structured_itineraries.variation_id': 4364}).all()])
    91

    >>> len([x for x in g.departures.filter(**{'tour_dossier.id': '22758'}).all()])
    0
```

3. 🤔 


## Expected result:

Order of operations shouldn't matter. We should see 280 departures for `tour_dossier.id=22758` and 91 for `structured_itineraries.variation_id=4364`.


## Investigation:

Looking at the `Query` class, it seems as though we clear `self._filters` after making _some_ requests, but not all:

- `get_resource_data` [resets it](https://github.com/gadventures/gapipy/blob/e91484bd2f99684d701bad54da5285104aeecad3/gapipy/query.py#L89) after doing `APIRequestor.get` (and caching the result)
- `count` [resets it](https://github.com/gadventures/gapipy/blob/e91484bd2f99684d701bad54da5285104aeecad3/gapipy/query.py#L165) after doing `APIRequestor.list_raw` (and extracting the count)
- `all` [calls `APIRequestor.list` but doesn't reset `self._filters`](https://github.com/gadventures/gapipy/blob/e91484bd2f99684d701bad54da5285104aeecad3/gapipy/query.py#L121-L141)


## Thoughts:

In _my_ opinion `Query.all` should reset `self._filters` after it calls `APIRequestor.list` and makes its request to the API -- adopting the same behaviour as the other two `Query` code paths which hit the API.

On the other hand, there is [a test case that _highlights_ the fact that we can "chain" filters in this way](https://github.com/gadventures/gapipy/blob/e91484bd2f99684d701bad54da5285104aeecad3/tests/test_query.py#L162).

This seems like an odd pair of behaviours to have (again, IMO):

- if I `.count()` my query the filters are reset
- if I `.all()` or `list(...)` my query the filters are not reset

---

NB: my contrived example could avoid this behaviour and obtain correct counts using `Query.count` rather than iterating over the list... it's easy to imagine similar analogous situations where e.g. whole resources and not counts are required

When rate limited by Conversation, the error thrown by the SDK is vague:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):

```


cURL reveals the actual service response to be this (as plain text):
```
Rate limit exceeded
```

```
{"error":"Maximum workspaces limit exceeded. Limit = 20"}
```

We are receiving this vague error for certain URLs when using Watson's NLU API. I saw in [a previously closed issue](https://github.com/watson-developer-cloud/python-sdk/issues/182) that the cause might be the site's robots.txt preventing Watson from scraping the URL. 

However, in our case, it doesn't seem to be the issue. Also, in cases when robots.txt was blocking Watson we've seen this, appropriate error message: "Request to fetch blocked: disallowed."  

Here is an example URL that yields this error: https://www.unionathletics.com/news/2017/5/1/womens-outdoor-track-field-bechu-collects-third-straight-liberty-league-performer-of-the-week-honors.aspx

And their robots.txt which doesn't seem like it should block Watson: https://www.unionathletics.com/robots.txt

Could someone explain what this error means: **Attempt to fetch failed: :closed**? Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks in advance,
Jason

_PS - I have noticed that this site wants a User-Agent set in the request. I checked and see that Watson does set the User-Agent string when fetching URLs to: "watson-url-fetcher", so, I don't think that's the issue, but maybe?_

issue_title: Allow developers to choose the return type ('json', 'xml') for services like "Relationship Extraction"
issue_body:
This is not an issue, I just wanted to bring this up as an enchancement: If you see the file "relationship_extraction_v1_beta.py", you can see that "'rt': 'xml'" is hardcoded.

This means that this service will ALWAYS return "XML" as its format. However, I prefer using JSON, and other developers have their own preferences, so I propose a solution: Just let the developer choose which Data Type they prefer! We can do this by adding "rt" or "return_type" as one of the parameters passed into the "extract" function of the class.

I have made a pull request https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/pull/66 in which I included automated testing. This will hopefully satisfy the requirement for the v2.0 roadmap https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/issues/40. A few notes regarding the PR:

1- I have used `pytest` as the test tool. It's better imo than Django Testing. The original tests have not been touched as `py.test` is compatible with them. Nonetheless, if you don't like it, automation can be done without it and I can edit the PR (see the examples on the pytest [website](http://pytest.org/latest/contents.html))

2- `pytest` is able to generate a `runtests` script which I did, so `python runtests.py` would run the tests without the user invoking `py.test` manually.

3- `Tox` has been used to test different environments. It's a great tool for such purposes and it goes well with `pytest` but it's not a necessary. (see [docs](https://tox.readthedocs.org/en/latest/)).

Now this is the worse part:

4- If we're to test a combination of `python2.7`, `python3.2`, `python3.3`, `python3.4`, `django1.6`, `django1.7`, `django1.8`, `drf2.4`, `drf3.0`, `drf3.1`, `drf3.2` Then only a handful of these tests will pass. Anything other than `python{27,33,34}, django18, drf{31,32}` will FAIL. I had a quick look at the failures, some of them are easy to fix and some are not. However, I didn't fix anything in order to follow a *branch per feature* process.

Finally, thanks to [drf-json-api](https://github.com/kevin-brown/drf-json-api) as I got the idea for automated testing from it. That project is **identical** to this but this is more mature and active and the code is quite clean (despite the fact that the above tests **all** work in *drf-json-api* excluding `drf2.4`) . Sad fragmentation in OSS.

issue_title: Should not assume that matplotlib is available
issue_body:
We currently pass `--matplotlib=inline` when starting the kernel, to force figures to appear inline. However, this means that if `matplotlib` is not installed in the environment where the kernel runs (which may not be the same as the one nbval is running in), the kernel will fail to start.

From comments on #6.

The keyword `#PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT` used to ignore comparison of computed and saved output originates from a time when the tool was called 'pytest-validate'. As the name now is 'nbval' (standing for NoteBook VALidation), it seems sensible to rename that keyword to `#NBVAL_IGNORE_OUTPUT`.

We should still allow #PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT to be used for backwards compatibility.

Must update documentation accordingly.

The way we're doing things at the moment is not the smartest I don't think. Given how slow development is it's not really an issue but `versioneer` seems to be a good option if it does become an issue. 

https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer

``` python
import luigi

    def requires(self):

    def complete(self):

By default, `sudo pip install --upgrade music21` installs the old version 1.9.3, even though version 2.0.0 is already [available on Pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/music21/2.0.0) and the `--upgrade` flag should force installation of the latest version. When run with multiple `-verbose` flags, pip's output has a bunch of lines like the following:

```
Not searching https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/releases/download/v2.0.0/music21-2.0.0.tar.gz#md5=60183b92885d7ed4176f712e4c8f75b3 (from https://pypi.python.org/simple/music21/) for files because external urls are disallowed.
```

So the issue seems to be that the tarball is externally hosted on Github and pip by default does not allow installation from remote locations. Indeed, the following command fixes the issue and updates to the latest version:

    sudo pip install --upgrade --allow-all-external music21

Can this be fixed somehow, e.g. by uploading the latest tarball to PyPI? 

One of the problems with the older version 1.9.3 is that IPython notebook integration is broken: `stream.show()` only gives a cryptic Javascript error instead of displaying an image of the notes. However, there is almost no way to trace the error back to the fact that it's not the latest version (it took me a good few hours to debug this, and I only discovered it by chance when comparing with a working installation on a different computer).

Many thanks!

https://github.com/celery/billiard/blob/master/billiard/connection.py#L404

The first time through the loop, if there is an exception which does not match `getattr(exc, 'errno', None) != errno.EINTR` then no error handling is done, `n` is not set, and this error occurs. 

Also, if it is not the first time through the loop, then `n` will be set, but from the previous time, so the code is very buggy in this case too.

issue_title: add option to ``lpad`` command to take into account the restrictions of a FireWorker
issue_body:
It would be useful to be able to restrict the ``lpad`` command to the jobs that are runnable by a particular FireWorker. Having a flag that allows you to set the path of a fireworker file, or just uses the default FWorker config, to further restrict the query on ``get_fws`` would be useful.

See also:

https://github.com/materialsproject/fireworks/issues/291

there isn't any way to set a subset of found lostruns to fizzle/rerun based on a query. 

@albalu

Quandl doesn't support Python 2.6 anyway, so there is no reason why to bother with adding an additional dependency on ``unittest2``.

[This patch](https://github.com/quandl/quandl-python/files/2283184/remove_unittest2.patch.txt) fixes the situation.

If I have a project (say, [Rhosydd](https://git.apertis.org/cgit/rhosydd.git/)), which contains two separate `hotdoc.json` files, documenting two separate libraries, and I build with `make -j5` to parallelise the build, the two `hotdoc` processes will fight over `$git/hotdoc-private/hotdoc.db`, as SQLite is not multi-writer-safe.

This generally manifests itself as the following failure from one of the `hotdoc` processes:
```OperationalError: (sqlite3.OperationalError) disk I/O error```

The workaround is to use a non-parallel build, i.e. with `make -j1`, or a `.NOTPARALLEL` rule. However, that’s slow and not fun.

Instead, hotdoc should use a per-configuration-file private directory, named after the `hotdoc.conf` file (which I think is the only unique thing we can guarantee to have), and ideally hidden so it doesn’t get in the way of tab completion in the source tree. Kind of like how autotools adds `.libs` and `.deps` subdirectories everywhere, which you hardly ever notice.

For example, for the configuration file `$srcdir/libcroesor/docs/hotdoc.json`, I would suggest a private folder of `$builddir/libcroesor/docs/.hotdoc.json-private`. For `$srcdir/src/libcroesor-hotdoc.json`, I would suggest a private folder of `$builddir/src/.libcroesor-hotdoc.json-private` or something like that. Something with a one-to-one mapping to configuration file names.

issue_title: C API: torque joint control doesn't work for MJCF, SDF
issue_body:
`loadMjcf()` and `loadSdf()` both call `processImportedObjects()`, which **creates motor for every joint**.

This is wrong, because if API user wants to uses `CONTROL_MODE_TORQUE`, he calls  `addJointTorqueMultiDof()`  that passes torque directly to Featherstone solver. Motor counteracts this torque in order to satisfy its uninitialized (zero) target and body **cannot move**.

TODO: enable motor only if `CONTROL_MODE_VELOCITY` or `CONTROL_MODE_POSITION_VELOCITY_PD` ever called for a joint.

contract ERC20Interface {

As I am moving those to funcserver. They don't make sense in the context of basescript

![basescript](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34322575/38681344-0c072fb4-3e86-11e8-9b0f-4fdadd1fd33e.png)
In version 0.2.1 It is printing all logs. 
In 0.2.4 It is not printing debug logs alone.

Useful for getting structured logs.



| Without basescript| 
| -------- | 
| ![](https://i.imgur.com/nsS0swI.png)|

| With basescript|
|--------------- |
| **stdout** | 
| ![](https://i.imgur.com/ZZ3TJix.png)|

|

### Install basescript
```
pip3 install basescript
```

#### Modifications in `settings.py`
```
from basescript import init_logger

MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',
    'django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware',
    'django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware',
    'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware',
    'django.contrib.auth.middleware.AuthenticationMiddleware',

]

init_logger(
	fmt = None,
	quiet = False,
        level = 'INFO',
        fpath = '/var/logs/web.log',
        pre_hooks = [],
        post_hooks = [],
        metric_grouping_interval = 1)
```

#### Arbitary `views.py`
```
from basescript import get_logger

log = get_logger()

 def signup(self, req: Request, username: str, email: str, password: str) -> str:
         
        #Add custom logs here 
        req.raw_request.log.info('sdsd')
        
        if not all([username, email, password]):
            raise Exception('Need all of username, email and password')
        user = User.objects.create_user(
            username=username, email=email, password=password)
        return user.id
```

#### Configuring middleware (optional)

##### Create a `middleware.py` : To override the default logger of django with custom logs using basescript

Example:
```
from basescript import get_logger

log = get_logger()

class LoggerMiddleware(object):
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response

    def __call__(self, request):
        user = request.user
        _log = log.bind(is_authenticated=bool(user.is_authenticated))
        if user.is_authenticated:
            _log = _log.bind(user=dict(username=user.username, id=user.id))
        request.log = _log
        return self.get_response(request)
```

same initNs in each time. A DAWG_DATASET_COMPLIANCE flag would not be a burden to 
that group. Other people would either ignore the setting (and use default or explicit 
graphs in all queries) or set it per-query if they have to.

issue_body:
Is this the wanted behavior?

```
>>> a=Article(_id=3, title="Test")
>>> a.to_dict()
{'title': 'Test'}
>>> a.meta.to_dict()
{'id': 3}
```

The index metadata will only be added when the object is saved (or more probably when the object is updated after saving).

However, this prevents us from accessing this data directly before the object is saved.

As it is now, we have to call e.g.:

```
>>> a._doc_type.index
'blog'
```

Personally, I wouldn't mind having it accessible through ```
a._index```
 before it is saved.
What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Circularity: kolibri.utils cannot depend on outside kolibri modules
issue_body:
## Summary

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/commit/9770e883d2f5ae418900aafed65d5080f6812e6e introduces this line in `kolibri.utils.server`

     from kolibri.deployment.default.wsgi import application 

...then in `kolibri.utils.cli` we have this written twice:

     # import from server.py here to avoid circular imports caused by importing kolibri.deployment.default.wsgi
     from . import server

The actual problem is when `kolibri.utils.server` starts depending on an outside environment at load-time. This behavior should be reserved for very explicit use in runtime calls to `kolibri.utils.cli`.

At least the problem is solved by moving the wsgi import statement from loadtime to somewhere runtime.

@66eli77 assigning you because you're working on daemon mode right now :)

see front page, which gives precedence to non-western alphabets:

issue_title: Enhance the webpack builder to look at INSTALLED_APPS for buildable assets
issue_body:
## Use case:

An external contributor does a `pip install kolibri` and then creates their own plugin with frontend JS.

After doing a `kolibri plugin enable` and `npm run build`, webpack should then inspect the list of `INSTALLED_APPS` (where the new plugin is presumably added), and then build assets, and inform Django that the new assets are available for serving.

issue_body:
### Observed behavior

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/33459811-408b67fa-d5e0-11e7-9a76-f92de9edc395.png)

### Expected behavior

not clipped

### Observed behavior

After setting `KOLIBRI_LISTEN_PORT=9090` in `/etc/kolibri/daemon.conf`, then starting via `service kolibri start` the server still tried to start on port 8080 (which is occupied on my system).

### Expected behavior

Kolibri should start on the port defined by `KOLIBRI_LISTEN_PORT` in `/etc/kolibri/daemon.conf`

### User-facing consequences

Can't start the server on an alternate port automatically using services

### Context

0.9.2

### Fix

The problem seems to be that the wrong variable name is getting exported in `/etc/default/kolibri` -- the following one-liner will fix the problem:

`sed -i '/export KOLIBRI_LISTEN$/ s/LISTEN$/LISTEN_PORT/' /etc/default/kolibri`

However I can't find this config file in the current repository to deliver a patch.

This is probably related to https://github.com/jquery/jquery/issues/2824

jQuery("#Курси") selector works. However, when I click on a link that has href set to "Курси", chrome (or some wordpress plugin, javascript library, not sure) automatically URL encodes it, and such a selector doesn't work:

jQuery("#%D0%9A%D1%83%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8")

And I get an error: Uncaught Error: Syntax error, unrecognized expression: #%D0%9A%D1%83%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8

I'm using jquery 1.12.4, jquery migrate 1.4.1, Chrome 63

Ref https://github.com/jquery/jquery/issues/2073#issuecomment-178087653

Warn on access of `jQuery.expr.filters` or `jQuery.expr[":"]`.

issue_title: can't perform operations between mx.sym.var and mx.nd.array

issue_body:
Following instructions from http://mxnet.io/get_started/ubuntu_setup.html
MXNet can't be built on Ubuntu 16.04 with the default OpenCV (version 2.4.9) because MXNet 0.9.5 requires at least OpenCV 3.x due to the reference to -lopencv_imgcodecs.
In addition, OpenCV3.1 is not compatible with CUDA8 so OpenCV3.2 is required.

Installing OpenCV3.2-dev following instructions from 
https://github.com/BVLC/caffe/wiki/OpenCV-3.1-Installation-Guide-on-Ubuntu-16.04
resolves the issue.

For bugs or installation issues, please provide the following information.
The more information you provide, the more likely people will be able to help you.

## Environment info
Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04

Compiler: gcc 5.4.0

MXNet version: 0.9.5

import mxnet as mx
import random

#synthetically create 1000 encoded sentences
encoded_sentences = []
for i in list(range(1000)):
    sentence_length = random.randint(1,20)
    sentence = random.sample(range(20), sentence_length)
    encoded_sentences.append(sentence)

#define hyperparameters/info
vocab = list(set(index for list in encoded_sentences for index in list))
batch_size = 18
num_embed = 10
num_hidden = 3
buckets = [5,10,15,20]
epochs = 15

#use mxnet bucketing iterator, label is next value in sentence
train_iter = mx.rnn.BucketSentenceIter(sentences = encoded_sentences,
                                       batch_size = batch_size,
                                       buckets = buckets,
                                       data_name = 'data',
                                       label_name = 'softmax_label')

#define recurrent cell outside of network TODO: why outside?
r_cell = mx.rnn.LSTMCell(num_hidden=num_hidden)

#define a network symbol based on sentence length
def sym_gen1(seq_len):

    print("-" * 50)

    #define input shape so we can infer layer shapes as we go
    data_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)
    label_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)

    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    print("\ndata shape: ", data.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label')
    print("\nlabel shape: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    embed = mx.sym.Embedding(data=data, input_dim=len(vocab), output_dim=num_embed,name='embed')
    print("\nembed layer shape: ", embed.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    output, shapes = r_cell.unroll(seq_len, inputs=embed, merge_outputs=True)
    print("\nconcatenated recurrent layer shape: ", output.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "after ", seq_len, " unrolls\n")

    reshape = mx.sym.Reshape(output, shape=(-1, num_hidden))
    print("\nafter reshaping: ", reshape.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    fc = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=reshape, num_hidden=len(vocab), name='pred')
    print("\nfully connected layer shape: ", fc.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Reshape(label, shape=(-1,))
    print("\nlabel shape after reshaping: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    pred = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc, label=label, name='softmax')
    print("\nsoftmax shape: ", pred.infer_shape(data=data_shape, softmax_label = label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    return pred, ('data',), ('softmax_label',)

#define identical symbol but remove name arguement from fully connected layer
def sym_gen2(seq_len):

    print("-" * 50)

    #define input shape so we can infer layer shapes as we go
    data_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)
    label_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)

    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    print("\ndata shape: ", data.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label')
    print("\nlabel shape: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    embed = mx.sym.Embedding(data=data, input_dim=len(vocab), output_dim=num_embed,name='embed')
    print("\nembed layer shape: ", embed.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    output, shapes = r_cell.unroll(seq_len, inputs=embed, merge_outputs=True)
    print("\nconcatenated recurrent layer shape: ", output.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "after ", seq_len, " unrolls\n")

    reshape = mx.sym.Reshape(output, shape=(-1, num_hidden))
    print("\nafter reshaping: ", reshape.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    fc = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=reshape, num_hidden=len(vocab))
    print("\nfully connected layer shape: ", fc.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Reshape(label, shape=(-1,))
    print("\nlabel shape after reshaping: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    pred = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc, label=label, name='softmax')
    print("\nsoftmax shape: ", pred.infer_shape(data=data_shape, softmax_label = label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    return pred, ('data',), ('softmax_label',)


#create a trainable bucketing module on cpu
model = mx.mod.BucketingModule(sym_gen=sym_gen1, default_bucket_key=train_iter.default_bucket_key, context=mx.cpu())
model_not_working = mx.mod.BucketingModule(sym_gen=sym_gen2, default_bucket_key=train_iter.default_bucket_key, context=mx.cpu())

#fit the first module
print("\n\n\nFITTING FIRST MODULE...\n\n\n")
metric = mx.metric.create('loss')
model.bind(data_shapes=train_iter.provide_data,
           label_shapes=train_iter.provide_label)
model.init_params()
model.init_optimizer(optimizer='Adam')
for epoch in range(epochs):
    train_iter.reset()
    metric.reset()
    for batch in train_iter:
        model.forward(batch, is_train=True)             # compute predictions
        model.backward()                                # compute gradients
        model.update()                                  # update parameters
        model.update_metric(metric, batch.label)        # update metric
    print('\n', 'Epoch %d, Training %s' % (epoch, metric.get()))

#fit the second module
print("\n\n\nFITTING SECOND MODULE...\n\n\n")
metric = mx.metric.create('loss')
model_not_working.bind(data_shapes=train_iter.provide_data,
           label_shapes=train_iter.provide_label)
model_not_working.init_params()
model_not_working.init_optimizer(optimizer='Adam')
for epoch in range(epochs):
    train_iter.reset()
    metric.reset()
    for batch in train_iter:
        model_not_working.forward(batch, is_train=True)             # compute predictions
        model_not_working.backward()                                # compute gradients
        model_not_working.update()                                  # update parameters
        model_not_working.update_metric(metric, batch.label)        # update metric
    print('\n', 'Epoch %d, Training %s' % (epoch, metric.get()))
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Run the script
2. You will see the module fit correctly with *gen_sym1()* but fail with *gen_sym2()*.  The only difference between the two is the name arguement has been removed from *fc = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=reshape, num_hidden=len(vocab), name='pred')*.

## What have you tried to solve it?

1. I have solved the problem by including an input to the name parameter when creating the symbol.

int MXNDArraySyncCopyFromCPU(NDArrayHandle handle,
const void *data,
size_t size)

issue_title: Ensure that all operators properly support kAddTo in backward pass
issue_body:
PR9495 fixes one operation where the backward pass of an operator did not obey the "req" argument properly (resp. not at all) and so ignored the "kAddTo" directive (which directs the operator to add the gradients to the output tensor(s) and not just assign them. This leads to wrong gradients in case that an operator fans out its output to more than one other operator.

We should examine all operators whether they all properly handle the "req" parameter in backward pass. There is at least one more that doesn't which is **svm_output.**

As this are basic and easy to fix but hard to detect problems (they may just slightly derail the training over time) we should really prioritize this sanity checking,

I used the trained model get two different size of feature map from two different sized image input. And now I want to do the cross-correlation operation (or convolution) of those two feature map. But I didn't find such an API in document. How can I do that ?

I am trying to run example https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/example/rnn/bucket_R , but seems like "mx.io.bucket.iter" is no longer available as I am getting error:

issue_title: Clarify behavior of shared executor memory sharing when using Bind
issue_body:
In the docs for MXExecutorBindEX in https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/doxygen/c__api_8h.html, the parameter `shared_exec` is somewhat cryptically described as "input executor handle for memory sharing".

I'm using `shared_exec` to do automated bucketing in Mathematica's deep learning framework, but what I'd like to know is what happens when I bind an executor that requires *more* memory than the memory used by the `shared_exec` that I passed in. 

The reason I need to know that is the following scenario: when more than one sequence is involved in a net, it is impossible to predict which bucket will need the maximum temporary memory (ahead of time). Therefore it is impossible to automatically pick a 'master executor' which will require more scratch mem than all other executors.

So here we really need to know what the semantics of `shared_exec` is! Can the `shared_exec` have *less* scratch than what is needed by the executor being bound? What happens in that case? Or do I need to pick a master executor and always pass it as the `shared_exec`? If that is the case, how can I know in advance whether my master executor needs to be retired in favor of another executor, if the only way to measure the scratch taken by an executor is after it is already created (and even then one has to parse a string to find the answer in megabytes, IIRC). 

@sbodenstein

issue_title: Broken link for "MXNet R Reference Manual"?
issue_body:
On the MXNet page for the R API (http://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/r/index.html) the link to the "MXNet R Reference Manual" seems to point to a document called "mxnet-test.pdf", whose contents are titled "MXNet Documentation Release 0.0.8" and seem to be for the Julia language, not R.

Is this link incorrect, or am I just confused?  I am looking for the MXNet R API.

Thanks.
